summary: Graham argues that hackers are returning to Macs because OS X combines Unix power with great industrial design, reviving the Mac’s original status as the hacker’s computer. He claims hacker preferences predict mainstream computing trends, so Apple’s renewed appeal among technical elites foreshadows broader adoption.
key_points: OS X makes Macs both Unix-strong and well-designed, drawing hardcore hackers back.
Hackers are a small but leading indicator for future mainstream tech adoption.
Great software starts on the platforms hackers personally use, which then drives hardware sales.
“Open” isn’t enough; platforms must be open and good to attract developers.
Apple’s current popularity at both low and high ends is likely temporary, implying market share growth.
money_quotes: Powerbooks are beautifully designed and run FreeBSD. What more do you need to know?
For most of us, it's not a switch to Apple, but a return.
When it comes to computers, what hackers are doing now, everyone will be doing in ten years.
If you want to attract hackers to write software that will sell your hardware, you have to make it something that they themselves use.
It's not enough to make it "open." It has to be open and good.
key_insight: Because hackers lead technological adoption, Apple’s resurgence among them signals broader future demand for Macs.
